coredata.dc:description: Among the various methods of medicines determinations, electrochemical techniques have attracted more interest. Carbon nanomaterials have commercialized and they have appeared as merchandises. The different structures of nanomaterials were applied in various electrochemical techniques and there are several introduced electroanalysis methods for pharmaceutical applications. Some of the carbon nanostructure-based electrodes and electrochemical cells are discussed here as important methods for pharmaceutical applications. The modeled carbon nanopillow, as an allotrope of carbon, was evaluated as well here.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 6
chapter.ce:title: Carbon nanomaterials for electroanalysis in pharmaceutical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among the various methods of medicines determinations, electrochemical techniques have attracted more interest. Carbon nanomaterials have commercialized and they have appeared as merchandises. The different structures of nanomaterials were applied in various electrochemical techniques and there are several introduced electroanalysis methods for pharmaceutical applications. Some of the carbon nanostructure-based electrodes and electrochemical cells are discussed here as important methods for pharmaceutical applications. The modeled carbon nanopillow, as an allotrope of carbon, was evaluated as well here.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical techniques have gained more interests due to their high selectivity, high sensitivity, low analyte concentration, cost-effective, portable and easy-to-use setup. In order to electrochemically detect chemical and biological samples, several devices such as photoelectrochemical sensors, electrochemical luminescence sensors, electrochemical impedance sensors, amperometric sensors, and voltammetric sensors were constructed and developed.

As it is well known, the electrode material plays an important role in the fabrication of an applicable electrochemical sensor. Generally, it enhances the electrocatalytical activity of the analyte at the surface of electrode and presents suitable conductivity. Various electrodes based on carbon paste, glassy-carbon, metallic plates (i.e., Pt, Cu, stainless steel, and nickel foam), conductive glasses (i.e., indium tin oxide (ITO), and fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO)) etc. can be used in electroanalytical study of pharmaceutical compounds.

Lately, nanomaterials introduce a new area in the modification of electrochemical systems, especially the electrode materials, which perfectly enhance the electroanalysis procedure and its results. Nanomaterials generally contain many great features in all areas of optical, electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties. However, in the case of electrochemical measurements, their unique structure and electrical conductivity has become enhanced due to the influence on electron transfer system.

Among different kinds of nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) such as graphene, carbon nanotube (CNT), fullerene, porous carbon, etc. have many unique features which makes them a promising candidate to be used as working electrode (WE) materials. They have superb electrical conductivity, high surface area, unique and tunable structure. This makes it possible to electrocatalytically improve the redox reaction of pharmaceutical compounds at the surface of WE and produce high sensitivity, high selectivity, and low detection limits.
In this chapter, we are about to focus on synthesis, characterization, and application of CNMs in the fabrication of electrochemical probes to detect and measure pharmaceutical compounds.

Various studies were done in order to review the electroanalytical capability of different devices to measure pharmaceutical compounds, such as Heli et al. who reviewed the nanostructured materials in electroanalysis of pharmaceuticals (Rahi et al., 2016) or carbon electrode material for biological applications was reviewed (Rivas et al., 2007; Ahammad et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2016). Recently developed screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) for application in pharmaceutical and biological analysis were reviewed in the work of Quinaz and coworkers (Couto et al., 2016), which mentioned the effective role of CNMs-based electrodes.

Carbon, the mysterious member of the P block on the periodic table, was always on top of scientist’s favorite materials due to its ability to form a wide range of compounds, mostly by sharing its four valence electrons. As it is well known, the field of organic chemistry is totally based on this element bonding with itself and other nonmetallic elements. The older allotropes of carbon: amorphous carbon, graphite, and diamond have been known for decades. The later ones have different crystalline structures and properties. In diamond, a carbon atom in the form of tetrahedral structure makes four single bonds (sp3 hybridization) with four other carbon atoms, which leads to a three-dimensional (3D) crystalline covalent framework. Nevertheless, graphite is made up of accumulating 2D graphene layers on top of each other, in the order which is shown in Fig. 6.1 . Carbon atoms in graphene monolayer are in sp2 hybridization, which also has van der Waals interaction with other layers in graphite crystalline lattice. These different structures led to almost totally opposite features. Graphite is an efficient electrical conductor whereas diamond is an insulator. Diamond is optically transparent and the hardest known natural material however, graphite is a soft opaque black material (Atkins, 2010).

Despite diamond and graphite, carbon has some other allotropes, called carbon nanostructures. They can be classified into two main groups, based on the types of covalent bonds between their carbon atoms (Georgakilas et al., 2015). Group one consists of the graphenic nanostructures—primarily made up of densely packed sp2 carbon atoms in a hexagonal honeycomb crystal lattice. This group includes graphene, graphenic nanosheets, CNTs, nanohorns, onion-like carbon (OLC) nanospheres, and carbon dots (C-dots). The simplest and basic member of this group is graphene which will be briefly discussed further. The other group of carbon nanostructures contain carbon atoms with both hybridization of sp3 and sp2 in various ratios and also mixtures of amorphous and graphitic regions, or sometimes dominant sp3 carbon atoms. To date, nanodiamond and some forms of carbon dots with nongraphitic structures are the only familiar members of this group. The main characteristic property of this group is that the graphene parts or monolayers (e.g., CNTs) are not counted as their building blocks.

Another way to categorize carbon nanoallotropes can be to settle on their morphological characteristics (Georgakilas et al., 2015). In this regard, one category contains nanostructures with empty internal spaces, such as fullerene, CNTs, and nanohorns. The other category contains robust nanostructures with no internal spaces, such as nanodiamonds, C-dots, OLC spheres and even graphene, as it also has no internal spaces.

Finally, the latest classification mode is based on the dimensionality of the carbon nanostructures (Georgakilas et al., 2015). This type of classification includes (1) 0D carbon nanoallotropes (e.g., fullerenes, OLC structures, C-dots, and nanodiamonds), (2) 1D carbon nanostructures (e.g., CNTs, carbon nanofibers (CNFs)), and (3) 2D nanostructures (e.g., graphene, graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), and few-layer graphenes (FLGs)).

Despite these classifications, all carbon nanoallotropes contain some common properties (due to the sp2 carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice), and some significant differences (due to the differences in sizes and shapes). Their similarities are on the levels of electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, chemical reactivity, and optical properties; their greatest differences relate to their dispersibility in organic solvents (Georgakilas et al., 2015).

To date, CNMs have been used in a variety of applications. However, in this study we are about to describe their efficiency to act as an electrode material during the electrochemical determination of pharmaceutical compounds. Generally, wide potential window, high chemical stability, low cost, and electrocatalytic properties against a variety of redox reactions are the great features of carbon-based electrodes, as most cited (McCreery, 2008). Therefore, there are a huge number of reports studying the application of CNMs in electrochemistry; include electroanalysis (Zhang et al., 2016), electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage (Zhang et al., 2015; Dai et al., 2012; Candelaria et al., 2012) (e.g., dye-sensitized solar cells, fuel cells, supercapacitors and batteries), optoelectronic devices (Jariwala et al., 2013), and spectroelectrochemistry (Wang et al., 2015), etc.
Some of the most-common used CNMs used in electroanalysis of pharmaceutical compounds will be discussed in this section.

The fullerene discovery was the beginning of study on carbon nanostructures. Fullerene crystal is a zero-dimensional (0D) form of carbon, having face centered cubic (FCC) structure (Fig. 6.2 ) (Kulkarni, 2015). Fullerene ball-shaped carbon molecules are at the corners and faces of a cube. Each fullerene molecule has a cage-like structure in which all the carbon atoms are set on the surface of a nearly spherical shape. Fullerenes can contain 60, 70, 78 or even more (specific or magic) number of carbon atoms. However, the 60-atom structure is the most stable and spherical in shape (Kulkarni, 2015). Generally, the number of carbon atoms in the fullerene family may vary from 30 to 300 (Abo-Hamad et al., 2016). However, some of these structures have never been synthesized and are just theoretical models (Taherpour, 2008).

For the first time, in 1985, Kroto, Smalley and Curl introduced a novel stable cluster containing 60 carbon atoms, which was called a buckminsterfullerene, and they received the Nobel Prize in 1996 for this discovery (Kroto et al., 1985). The name Buckminsterfullerene was defined to the C60 molecule in honor of R. Buckminster Fuller, an American architect who designed geodesic domes with similar topologies (Terrones, 2004). The C60 molecule is made up 60 sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (a graphene sheet) curved in a spherical way by the introduction of pentagonal rings, as in a soccer ball (truncated icosahedron). Generally, according to Euler’s Law (Eq. (6.1)):
(6.1)
V + F – E = 2
where V, F and E are the number of vertices, faces and edges, respectively, only 12 pentagons are needed to close a graphene sheet completely (Terrones, 2004). For example, in C60 (12 pentagons and 20 hexagons), V=60, F=32 and E=90 with a confinement of no two pentagons touched each other and each pentagon shared an edge with hexagon; so-called isolated pentagon rule (IPR) (Kulkarni, 2015; Terrones, 2004). However, lower fullerenes do not obey IPR, they are non-IPR structures.

Huffman and Krätschmer produced milligram quantities of C60 in an arc discharge between graphite electrodes under helium atmosphere (Kratschmer et al., 1990). Since then, various studies all over the world were done to synthesize fullerenes. Laser ablation, hydrocarbon combustion, sputtering, and electron beam evaporation of graphite targets have been used to synthesis fullerene-based products (Lieber and Chen, 1994).

The fullerenes could be crystallized in a solid form. Smaller clusters of carbon atoms (<60 atoms) were also detected by means of mass spectrometry but are not stable and it is not possible to collect (Kulkarni, 2015). A fullerene cage can be chemically functionalized or impregnated by some ions which manipulate the fullerene molecules and tune its properties (Kulkarni, 2015; Lebedeva et al., 2015). Further modifications can be achieved through the combination of fullerene cages with different materials or nanomaterials. These are known to create or enhance the functional properties of final composition in all areas of electrical conductivity, intense light absorption, thermal conductivity, magnetism, mechanical strength and catalytic activity (Lebedeva et al., 2015). This route provides the preparation of materials presenting hybrid properties and retaining the specified characteristics of each single building block.

To date, a lot of fullerene applications were found in fields such as electronic devices (Kulkarni, 2015; Lebedeva et al., 2015), sensors (Yeow and Sinha, 2009), spintronic (Lin et al., 2011) and photovoltaic (Li et al., 2012) devices, and in medical applications such as drug delivery (Bolskar, 2012), imaging (Chen et al., 2012), and radiotherapy (Orlova et al., 2013).
Conversely, one of the most important point of studies on fullerene was the discovery of CNTs which will be discussed in the following sections.

On 1991, Iijima prepared nanometer-size, needle-like tubes of carbon, now called nanotubes, using an arc-discharge evaporation method (i.e., same used to produce fullerene) (Iijima, 1991). CNTs are considered rolls of graphite sheets, mostly closed at the ends, which are capped with hemispheres of fullerenes (containing six pentagons and different number of hexagons to properly fit on the tubes) during their formation (Kulkarni, 2015). CNTs are made up of sp2 carbon units and they present a seamless structure with hexagonal honeycomb lattices which are totally arranged in a helical fashion about the needle axis (Iijima, 1991; Zhao et al., 2002). Their tubular structures are typically several nanometers in diameter and many microns in length. They are divided in three families: multi-walled (MWCNTs), double-walled (DWCNTs) or single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) (Fig. 6.3 ). A SWCNT is constructed of a seamlessly rolled single graphite sheet, which is an individual cylinder with the diameter less than 3nm. MWCNTs are built of concentric and closed 2–50 graphite tubules (0.334nm distanced), formed with a range of diameters, typically from 2 to 25nm (Kulkarni, 2015; Iijima, 1991; Zhao et al., 2002).

As mentioned above, CNTs are considered as folding of a graphite sheet. In this regard, three types of CNTs are possible: armchair, zigzag, and helical (Fig. 6.4 ), according to their growth conditions. Zigzag and armchair CNTs are “achiral” tubes, i.e., their mirror images appear similar to their original structure; however, helical tubes are “chiral” and their mirror images are different from the original structure (Kulkarni, 2015). Besides these basic CNT morphologies, a variety of shapes, such as ropes, stripes, springs, bamboo structures, hollow-tube, herringbone, conical shapes, etc. have been formed, depending on different experimental conditions (Georgakilas et al., 2015; Kulkarni, 2015; Wildgoose et al., 2006).

Three most common CNT preparation methods are: arc discharge (Iijima, 1991), laser ablation (Thess et al., 1996), and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) (Wang et al., 2010) which were numerously studied and optimized to increase the yield and even to obtain SWCNTs. Investigations show that electric arc discharge can mostly produce MWCNTs, however laser ablation can beneficially lead to SWCNTs (Kulkarni, 2015). Furthermore, some reports represented the attempts of using ion beams to produce CNTs (Kulkarni, 2015; Yamamoto et al., 1996).

CNTs have some great advantages, such as high surface area (1315m2/g (Pumera, 2009)), small size, high electrical conductivity, high sensitivity and chemical stability, which makes them highly beneficial for verity of applications such as, hydrogen energy storage (Jones et al., 1997), catalyst support (Rezaeifard and Jafarpour, 2014; Navidi et al., 2013), electronic devices (Terrones, 2004; Wang et al., 2013), and especially favorable in sensor applications (Zhao et al., 2002). Fast responding, good reversibility, and high electron transfer, make CNTs highly beneficial to be used as electrode material in electrochemical reactions. Accordingly, numerous studies concerning CNT-based electrochemical sensors have been reported (Zhao et al., 2002; Wildgoose et al., 2006; De Volder et al., 2013). Lately, electrochemical sensors have been developed by employing functionalized CNTs (Gao et al., 2012). Furthermore, several studies demonstrated the use of small organic molecules, polymers, biomolecules, metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, and semiconductor nanoparticles to successfully modify CNTs and enhance their electrochemical activities (Rivas et al., 2007; Ahammad et al., 2009; Couto et al., 2016).

Graphene is a 2D nanomaterial constructed of carbon atoms densely packed into a benzene-ring structure (Fig. 6.5 ) (Novoselov et al., 2004). This honeycomb (hexagonal) crystal lattice with sp2-hybridized carbon atoms is counted to be the building block of other carbon allotropes; namely wrapped in 0D fullerenes, rolled up into 1D nanotubes, and stacked in 3D graphite (Pumera, 2009). It was firstly presumed that planar graphene does not exist in the free state, due to the lake of thermodynamic stability, until it was observed and characterized by the work of Geim and Novoselov in 2004 (Novoselov et al., 2004). They gained the 2010 physics Noble prize (6 years later) because of this discovery. Since then, graphene has been used in numerous fields, from optoelectronic and energy applications till sensors and biology (Georgakilas et al., 2016; Choi et al., 2010; Shamsipur et al., 2016) due to its many extraordinary features, including the electrical, optical, thermal and mechanical properties (Pumera, 2009). The important point is that all these unique properties are integrated in one material.

Graphene sheets are primarily stacked in two forms: ABA (Bernal stacking) and ABC (Fig. 6.6 ). The stacked graphene sheets, with the thickness of 2–10 layers are called graphene nanoplatelets (Pumera, 2009). However, the material with the thickness of 100 or even more graphene layers presents the electronic properties of graphite and is considered as thin layers of 3D graphite (i.e., highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) which will be discussed in Section 6.2.4.8).

Graphene is a semimetal because the valence and conduction bands are touching, and the overlap is zero (Guo and Dong, 2011). Furthermore, in its lattice, electrons can travel with only little scattering. The carrier mobility (μ in units of m2/V per s) of graphene monolayer was measured to be 200,000cm2/V per s (Bolotin et al., 2008) which results in its superior conductivity (σ in units of S/m) of this nanomaterial, related to Eq. (6.2):
(6.2)
σ = n
e
μ
where, n is the carrier density (m−3) and e is the elementary charge (1.6022×10−19 C). It was suggested that the quasi-free π electron in graphene basal plane is responsible for this high electrical conductivity. Conversely, graphene is the thinnest material with super high specific surface area of 2630m2/g (Pumera, 2009). These two basic properties make this material highly desirable to be used in electrodes’ construction especially for electrochemical measurements.

Among the various reported methods of graphene preparation, two main strategies are obvious; top-down approach (mainly decomposition of graphite or CNTs) and bottom-up approach (usually synthesis from hydrocarbon gases) (Pumera, 2009). The first report of FLG was the mechanical exfoliation of HOPG with scotch tape and deposition on to a silicon substrate (Novoselov et al., 2004). Although it can prepare high quality and monolayer graphene, the method is neither high-throughput nor high-yield (Allen et al., 2010). HOPG or graphite can be dispersed in various organic solutions/inorganic-surfactant solutions and ultrasonicated for several hours (Zhang et al., 2016). Exfoliation of graphite oxide into graphene oxide (GO) with further thermal (Becerril et al., 2008), microwave assisted (Zhu et al., 2010), chemical (Dong et al., 2010), or electrochemical (Shao et al., 2010) reduction can also lead to graphene platelets (Pumera, 2009).

Furthermore, various physical methods such as epitaxial growth on an insulator surface such as SiC (Berger et al., 2004), arc discharge between graphite electrodes under almost high pressure of H2 (Subrahmanyam et al., 2009), unzipping MWCNTs by plasma etching (to prepare GNRs) (Jiao et al., 2009) and CVD on surfaces of single crystals of metals (e.g., Si) (Shang et al., 2008) have been employed. The latter has the potential of producing high quality graphene with a scale-up approach using hydrocarbon gases as precursors, which has proved to be suitable for electrochemical applications (Zhang et al., 2016; Pumera, 2009; Guo and Dong, 2011).

There is a fullerene-type graphitic (multishell spherical) nanoallotrope which is called OLC (Georgakilas et al., 2015). These nanostructures are built up concentric graphenic shells (like giant fullerenes); see Fig. 6.7 . OLCs were primarily identified by strong electron beam irradiation of CNTs mixture by Ugarte (1992).

C-dots are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles containing high oxygen contents (Fig. 6.8 ) (Georgakilas et al., 2015). They consist of various volumetric ratios of graphitic and turbostratic carbon. C-dots present relatively strong photoluminescence (PL) property, depending on their size (diameter 2–10nm), the excitation wavelength, and the surface functionalization (Ming et al., 2012). The result of X-ray diffraction (XRD) study indicated that they have both amorphous and graphitic components (Ming et al., 2012). In addition, Raman spectroscopy demonstrates that C-dots have high contents of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms (Ming et al., 2012). Various methods have been developed to prepare C-dots, such as laser ablation (Sun et al., 2006), electrochemical methods (Ming et al., 2012), incomplete combustion of candle soot (Liu et al., 2007), and hydrothermal reaction (Sun and Li, 2004).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with quasi-cyclic crystal structures are obtained from cutting a graphene monolayer into small pieces in dimensions of 2–20nm and they mainly consist of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (Fig. 6.9 ) (Georgakilas et al., 2015; Ponomarenko et al., 2008). GQDs usually contain a few stacked graphene layers based on their graphenic precursors (Ponomarenko et al., 2008). Despite large graphene nanosheets (GNs), which have zero-width band gaps, GQDs have nonzero band gaps because of quantum confinement and edge effects (Ponomarenko et al., 2008). The band gaps of GQDs can be tuned by changing their size (smaller in size, larger band gap), shape, and geometry, chemical functionalization and the nature of their edges. GQDs also present strong PL, which shifts in parallel with tuning the band gap size (Peng et al., 2012). There are reports of GQDs in different applications such as cellular imaging (Zhang et al., 2012), photocatalysis (Zhuo et al., 2012), photovoltaic devices (Li et al., 2011), and phototransistors (Konstantatos et al., 2012).

Nanodiamonds are sp3-hybridized carbon nanoparticles with diameters less than 20nm that consist of crystal domains and diamondoid-like topology (Fig. 6.10 ) (Williams, 2011). As the diameter increases, the ratio of carbon atoms located at the surface decreases. Generally, half of the carbon atoms in nanodiamonds are located at the surface and are able to form bonds with hydrogen or other organic functional groups affecting the structures stability. Nanodiamond properties resemble those of organic molecules rather than bulk diamonds. They are naturally obtained in petroleum deposits (Dahl et al., 2003) and prepared in methods such as ball milling (Niwase et al., 1995), thermionic vacuum arc (Musa et al., 2004), or abrasion of micro-diamonds (Jean-Paul et al., 2009). Nanodiamonds consist of tetrahedral sp3 carbon atoms, whereas, C-dots contain both types of sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in various ratios.

Carbon nanopillow (with the structures in Fig. 6.11 ) is an allotrope of carbon which was modeled by our team. It is constructed from two graphene basal planes in top and bottom, linked together with 4- and 8-carbon rings in edges. This type of designed structure of carbon nanopillow was introduced here for first time by the authors of this chapter. The carbon nanopillow with only 6-carbon rings was modeled by Bosson et al. (2010). However, our study demonstrates that the presence of 4-carbon rings on the edges and especially in corners perform steric tension in the material to be opened in various expected applications, such as drug delivery, energy storage devices (e.g., batteries), and adsorbent and filtration technology. Doping, impregnating, and releasing different ions and molecules are possible in carbon nanopillows through either 8-carbon rings on the edges or opening 4-carbon rings (especially in corners). It is predicted as a playground for storage or adsorption of a huge types of ions, molecules, compounds, particles, etc., which makes it highly beneficial in many science areas such as pharmaceutical analysis and drug delivery. Furthermore, modification and hybridization of this carbon nanoallotrope is expected to be possible, even easier than graphene, but with notable results.

CNFs (Fig. 6.12 ) are known as a noncontinuous 1D carbon nanostructures with cylindrical or conical shape (diameter 50–200nm), which consist of various arrangement of stacked and curved graphene sheets (Ramos et al., 2013). These nanostructures were identified more than a century before the discovery of CNTs. Depending on the way of arranging GNs, several types of CNFs may exist: (1) platelet, (2) herringbone (or fishbone), (3) ribbon (or tubular), (4) stacked-up and cone- stacked, and (5) cone-helix CNFs (Ramos et al., 2013). However, CNFs don’t have the hollow cavity, which may be counted as a main difference with CNTs. CNFs are produced in catalytic thermal CVD (Martin-Gullon et al., 2006), plasma-enhanced CVD (Melechko et al., 2005), and electrospinning (Chronakis, 2005).

GNRs can be described as a 1D sp2-hybridized carbon strip of finite dimension with defined edges (Peng et al., 2012). Carbon atoms in GNRs are of non3-coordinated nature. There are three types of currently known GNRs depending on the edge termination; armchair, zigzag, and a combination of both (hybrid GNRs) (Fig. 6.13 ) (Terrones et al., 2010; Tao et al., 2011). The edge patterns in armchair GNRs are stable due to their strong dangling bonds; however, in zigzag GNRs, edge reconstructions are expected at elevated temperatures. In order to stabilize the edge structure, hydrogen saturation or (rarely) pentagonal and heptagonal carbon rings may be applied (Terrones et al., 2010). Similar to graphene, GNRs may contain bilayer or few-layer arrangements; in the presence of more stacked layers of finite graphene strips, the structure is termed as graphitic nanoribbon (Terrones et al., 2010). Most studies of producing GNRs have focused on unzipping CNTs (Sinitskii et al., 2010).

Natural graphite has an imperfect structure because of defects and inclusions. Pyrolytic graphite is a family member of graphite but with a high degree of crystallographic orientation. The abbreviation of HOPG means highly-ordered high-purity pyrolytic graphite (Fig. 6.14 ). It comprises a class of most-ordered 3D carbon materials and is considered paralleled stacked graphene sheets. Generally, HOPGs are layered polycrystals and each bulk polycrystal build blocks of microscopic monocrystal grains with different sizes. HOPG is composed of paralleled basal plane layers, and a perpendicular edge plane to basal plane. In each layer of HOPG, strong covalent bonds exist; however, there are weak van der Waals forces between different layers. High purity HOPG can be prepared from pyrolytic graphite at high temperatures and pressures using gaseous hydrocarbons (McCreery, 2008). HOPG exhibits the anisotropic nature which causes different thermal conductivity and electrical resistivity in different directions. This structure is highly desirable for substrates in scanning electron microscopy, monochromators in X-ray (Berger et al., 1970; Pease et al., 2000) and neutron radiation (Bergsma and Dijk, 1967), and electrochemical analysis. High density of edge planes in fabricated electrodes is responsible for a rapid electron transfer rate due to presence of defect sites desirable for electrocatalysis and electroanalysis (Zhang et al., 2016).

Porous carbon materials have remarkable features, such as high specific surface areas, large pore volumes, and high chemical and mechanical stability which makes them useful in various fields, such as water and air purification, chemical and electrochemical catalysis, chromatography, and energy storage (Knox et al., 1986; Nishihara and Kyotani, 2012; Ryoo et al., 2001; Munyentwali and Zhu, 2015; Roberts et al., 2014). Generally, porous materials are categorized into three groups, depending on their pore width; microporous (<2nm), mesoporous (2–50nm), and macroporous (>50nm). Various procedures can be applied to prepare porous carbon materials with different pore sizes and pore structures. Porous carbons are usually prepared via carbonization followed by activation (Ryoo et al., 2001). Ordered microporous carbon structures have been prepared using zeolites as templates. Nevertheless, ordered macroporous carbons are synthesized by colloidal crystal templates. Mesoporous carbons with a disordered pore structure have been prepared through methods such as catalytic activation with metal species, carbonization (of polymer/polymer blends or organic aerogels), and template-assisted procedures using silica nanoparticles. Ordered mesoporous carbons (OMCs) containing various pore structures can be synthesized employing mesoporous silica templates include MCF, HMS, MSU-X, MCM-48, and SBA-15 (Fig. 6.15 ). OMCs were successfully applied as electrode materials in batteries, supercapacitors, fuel cells, and even electrochemical sensors for pharmaceutical compounds due to their well-ordered pore structures, high density of edge planes, high specific surface areas, and large pore volumes.

As it is well known, there are a large number of carbon allotropes which have been synthesized or theoretically predicted to date; however, in this work we were focused on most-cited ones with experimentally proven applications. Indeed, we apologize for unintentionally not mentioning any other carbon allotropes of this category.

In order to analyze various pharmaceutical and medicinal compounds, different analytical methods have been created and developed to date. However, they may not have all advantages such as accuracy, portability, rapid response, and low cost, simultaneously. In contrast, electrochemical methods are able to provide a simple and inexpensive path for in situ detection and determination of a wide variety of materials, even in complex matrixes with high sensitivity and precision (Rabieh et al., 2015).

Electrochemistry is a field of chemistry concerned with the interrelation of electrical and chemical effects (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). In other words, it is a system in which chemical composition can be changed by applying electron flow or, vice versa (Pumera, 2010). In a major part of this field, the study of chemical changes caused by the passage of an electric current and the generation of electrical energy by chemical reactions take place. Generally, this field enfolds various phenomenon (e.g., electrophoresis and corrosion), devices (electrochromic displays, electrochemical sensors, batteries), and other technologies (the electroplating of metals) (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Electrochemical measurements are applied to chemical systems for reasons such as investigating thermodynamic phenomenon of a reaction, producing and kinetic study of an unstable intermediate (e.g., a radical ion), studying the electrochemical properties of a system and measuring trace amounts of metal ions or organic species in a solution (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). In electrochemical systems, researchers are concerned with charge transport across the interface of electrode/electrolyte and the effective processes and factors on it (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). This is made possible by the movement of electrons (and holes).

An electrochemical cell generally consists of two electrodes and at least one electrolyte phase in between (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Generally, these cells can be classified as (1) electrolytic, electricity consumers from an external source, or (2) galvanic, electrical energy producers (Bard and Faulkner, 2001; Wang, 2006). Electrochemical reactions take place on the surfaces of an electronic conductor immersed in an electrolyte, which is called electrode (Pumera, 2010; Sato, 1998). The conducting material of an electrode may be electronically a metal or a semiconductor (Sato, 1998). Conventional electrode materials consist of solid metals (e.g., Pt), liquid metals (amalgams), carbon (graphite), and semiconductors (Si); they will be briefly discussed further in this section. Moreover, the electrolytes used are aqueous solution (of ions e.g., H+, Na+ and Cl−), fused salt (e.g., molten NaCl-KCl eutectic), solid electrolyte (sodium j8-alumina), or gaseous electrolyte (gaseous plasma) (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). The movement of ions in the electrolyte phase can carry the charge. For better electrochemical performance, the resistance of solvent/electrolyte system must be sufficiently low. Two main categories of electrochemical cells consist of two-electrode cell and three-electrode cell. The overall chemical reaction occurring in a cell is the combination of two independent half-reactions, describing the real phenomena at the two electrodes (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Each half-reaction is the result of the interfacial potential difference at the related electrode. Mostly, only one of these reactions is interested and the electrode at which it happens is called the WE. To standardize the other half of the cell, an electrode (called a reference electrode (RE)), consisting of phases with essentially constant composition, is employed (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and silver-silver chloride (Ag/AgCl/KCl(sat.)) are three conventional REs. In large-scale electrochemical cells or in measurements with nonaqueous solutions (lake of conductivity), a three-electrode cell is preferable (Fig. 6.16 ). In this set-up, the current is transmitted between the WE and a counter electrode (CE). The electrochemical properties of CE (or auxiliary electrode) do not affect the behavior of the WE, so it can be any convenient one (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). However, the CE must not produce substances by electrolysis that will reach the surface of the WE and cause interfering reactions there. The potential difference between the WE and the RE is measured by a device which has a high input impedance, so that an insignificant current is drawn through the RE (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). This three-electrode set-up is used in most electroanalytical experiments which will discuss in this chapter.

As it mentioned previously, WE is the key component of an electrochemical cell, therefore, its chemical and electrocatalytic properties can play an effective role in the performance of an electrochemical device, e.g., electrochemical sensors and biosensors. In this regard, various types of electrodes are used in these analytical devices. Since the electrochemical reactions take place at the surface of the electrode, two main strategies of surface activation and surface modification can successfully affect the performance of the interested electrode. Chemical or electrochemical surface activation can: (1) remove the surface contaminant, (2) increase the surface functional group, (3) increase the electrode surface area, and (4) expose a fresh surface to enhance the electrode behavior (Hu et al., 1985). In the case of electrode modification, various types of materials can be helpful, as mentioned before; however, in recent decades nanotechnology made a revolution to this area. For electroanalysis application, high sensitivity, reusability, reversibility, and ability to examine real samples are mainly desired reasons of electrode modification. CNMs as the pioneers of nanosized materials are always the focus of scientists since their discovery (Rivas et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2016; McCreery, 2008; Shao et al., 2010). Low overpotential, high current density, enhanced electrocatalytic activity, high sensitivity, and appropriate recovery are some of the great features of CNM-based modified electrodes, which made them enough convenient to measure various analytes (Abo-Hamad et al., 2016).

Various types of WEs can be used in electroanalysis procedure. The most common electrodes which were used for pharmaceutical analysis are categorized and introduced in this section and the CNM-based modified electrodes in next one.

While reviewing electroanalytical techniques, it was found that the earliest voltammetry (especially polarography) is coupled with mercury electrodes, due to their beneficial advantages such as high overpotential toward the proton reduction, wide cathodic potential range, amalgam formation, and renewable surface (Wang, 2006; Harvey, 2000; Heyrovský and Kůta, 1965). The mercury WEs often consist of a drop suspended from the end of a capillary tube (Fig. 6.17 ). In the hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE), a desirably sized Hg drop is formed through pushing it through a narrow capillary tube by a micrometer screw, which makes the suspended drop (Harvey, 2000). In the dropping mercury electrode (DME), successive mercury drops form at the end of a capillary tube resulting from gravity; each drop provides a fresh electrode surface (Harvey, 2000). In the static mercury drop electrode (SMDE), a solenoid-driven mechanical plunger is used to control the flow of mercury and each drop provides a fresh electrode surface (Harvey, 2000). The SMDE can be applied as either an HDME or as a DME. A mercury film electrode is built up of a thin layer of mercury on the surface of a solid carbon, Pt, or Au electrode, in which the solid electrode is immersed in a solution of Hg2+ and held at a potential (reduction potential of Hg2+ to Hg), to form a thin mercury film (Harvey, 2000).

Carbon paste electrodes (CPEs) have become an interesting tool in electroanalytical procedures (Shamsipur et al., 2016). They are basically prepared by filling a cavity at the end of an inert support with a paste consisting of carbon particles (commonly graphite) and organic viscous liquid for binding purposes (Fig. 6.18 ) (Abo-Hamad et al., 2016; Harvey, 2000). These electrodes have some advantages, such as such as feasibility to incorporate with different substances, low background currents, easy renewal, and composite nature (Rubianes and Rivas, 2003). Modification of CPEs with CNMs has caused highly improved electrocatalytic performance, more reversible redox activity, and reduced overvoltage effect compared to ordinary CPEs (Abo-Hamad et al., 2016; Rubianes and Rivas, 2003; Gasnier et al., 2013; Parvin, 2011). Moreover, it is expected that functionalized CNMs can further improve the electrochemical performance of modified CPEs (Abo-Hamad et al., 2016).

Glassy (or vitreous) carbon (GC) has distinguished mechanical and electrical properties, wide potential window, chemical inertness, and almost reproducible performance. It is prepared during a controlled heating program (300–1200°C) of a premodeled phenolformaldehyde resin in an inert atmosphere with elimination of oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen contaminants (Wang, 2006). Structurally, GC involves thin, tangled ribbons of cross-linked graphite-like sheets with small-sized pores and high density (Wang, 2006). Generally, in order to activate the surface of bare GC electrode (GCE) and enhance its analytical performance, it is successively polished with small particles (down to 0.05µm) of alumina or diamond until a mirror-like surface is achieved, followed by ultrasonication in a water bath to remove the abraded carbon contaminant and the polishing material (Wang, 2006; Hu et al., 1985). Further activation methods include chemical, electrochemical, thermal, plasma, and oxygen treatments are also employed for specific usages such as contaminant removal, surface functionalization, or exposure of fresh sites for electron transfer.

The carbon ceramic electrodes (CCEs) are composite electrodes made from fixing a silica matrix (obtained in sol-gel process) with powdered carbon (e.g., graphite, CNMs and GC) (Skeika et al., 2011). These electrodes were firstly described by Lev et al. (Tsionsky et al., 1994) and present a variety of advantages such as being rigid (unlike CPE), porous, high electrical conductive, getting easily modified, and having a renewable external surface (unlike GCE). CCEs can be employed for sensing different types of compounds and pharmaceutical analysis applying electrochemical techniques (Skeika et al., 2011; Thenmozhi and Sriman Narayanan, 2007; Habibi and Jahanbakhshi, 2015). The modification of these electrodes can provide new features, such as lowering the overpotential of analyte electrochemical behavior and further increasing the sensitivity of the introduced electrochemical sensor (Skeika et al., 2011; Habibi and Jahanbakhshi, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphite rod or pencil graphite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Another cheap and highly applicable carbon-based electrode is graphite rod or pencil graphite electrode (PGE) which was successfully used for the preparation of DNA hybridization biosensor (Alipour et al., 2016), determination of pharmaceutical compounds (Fard et al., 2016; Alipour et al., 2013), and other electrochemical devices (e.g., batteries).

Metallic electrodes are constructed using platinum, gold, silver, or copper which may be used over a wide range of potentials. As an example, a Pt electrode has the potential range of about +1.2 to –0.2V versus the SCE in acidic solutions, and from +0.7 to −1V versus the SCE in basic solutions (Harvey, 2000). Generally, solid electrodes beside CPEs are fashioned into disks that are sealed into the bottom of an inert support and are linked with an electrical lead (Fig. 6.19 ). Despite many good advantages of metallic electrodes, they have some problems, such as alternation of the electrode surface by the adsorption of electrolyte species or the formation of oxide layers. To solve these problems, the electrodes need to be frequently reconditioned through physical, chemical, or even electrochemical polishing.

A SPE is a chemically stable substrate on which one to three or even more electrodes (such as the WE, the pseudoreference electrode and the CE) are printed by screen-printing process (Fig. 6.20 ) (Couto et al., 2016). During the screen-printing process, layer-by-layer deposition of various inks at a solid substrate has been taken place. Screen-printing techniques provides a useful device in order to do in situ analysis of different compounds, especially in the case of pharmaceutical samples (Couto et al., 2016; Tudorache and Bala, 2007). They were reviewed in the reports from Bala (Tudorache and Bala, 2007), Banks (Metters et al., 2011) and Couto (Couto et al., 2016), considering their application on the electroanalysis of pharmaceutical, biological, environmental and food samples. SPEs are typically 3×1×0.05cm, however these dimensions can vary due to the material used and electrodes applications, which can also contain an array of electrodes packing in an insulating matrix (Grieshaber et al., 2008). As of the advantages of these electrodes, stability, reproducibility, uniformity, controllable dimensions, and suitable mass-transport rate can be mentioned here (Tudorache and Bala, 2007; Grieshaber et al., 2008). To produce an applicable SPE, different factors must be considered, such as geometry and size of SPE, which is controlled by screen, ink type, substrate material, printing and drying (Couto et al., 2016). As an insulating substrate in SPEs, various materials such as plastic, ceramic, polymer films, glass, etc. can be used (Couto et al., 2016).

Carbon ink or other carbon structures such as graphite (Gómez-Mingot et al., 2011), graphene (Ping et al., 2012; Zang et al., 2013), fullerene (Saeedfar et al., 2013), and CNTs (Asturias-Arribas et al., 2014) are of the most commonly used material in order to modify WE in SPEs. Moreover, other materials such as Au (Liu et al., 2010) and Pt (Wei et al., 2012) inks were also used in WEs of SPEs. Furthermore, various materials can be used in order to modify the surface of WE for electrochemical detection of different compounds. Ag/AgCl is the most commonly employed material to act as a pseudoreference electrode due to its stability and simple construction (da Silva et al., 2014). A CE may be made of the same material as a WE or an external platinum wire or rod can be utilized in alternative. Moreover, the surface of SPEs can be modified by polymers, Schiff-bases, or biomolecules, to provide higher electrochemical selectivity (Asturias-Arribas et al., 2014).

In general, two types of modification of SPEs are possible. First, variation of the composition of the inks with the use of different substances, and second, by modifying the prepared SPEs through depositing preferable substances on the surface of the electrodes which can enhance the electrodes’ performance by increasing its conductivity and selectivity (Couto et al., 2016).

Thin films of transparent conducting oxide (TCO) consist of binary and ternary compounds such as SnO2, In2O3, SnO2:Sb, SnO2: F, In2O3: Sn, Cd2SnO4, CdIn2O4, etc., which are used in practical transparent electrode applications such as liquid crystal displays, solar cells, and spectroelectrochemistry (Minami, 2000). Different kinds of transparent materials can act as a support for these films, such as glass, quartz, and even conducting polymers (e.g., polyethylene terephthalate, PET). Modification of these electrodes with CNMs (Fig. 6.21 ) can enhance their conductivity and electrocatalytic property toward the electrochemical behavior of different compounds and pharmaceutical compounds.

In some of the electrochemical techniques, movement of the electrode with respect to the solution is required. In such systems, the electrode is itself in motion (e.g., rotating disks, rotating wires, vibrating electrodes, etc.) or the solution forcedly flows past a stationary electrode. The rotating disk electrode (RDE) (Fig. 6.22 ) is made of a disk of electrode material (e.g., glassy carbon or platinum) embedded in a rod of an insulating material (e.g., glass, Teflon, epoxy resin, or another plastic). The electrode is attached to a motor and rotated at a certain frequency, f=ω/2π, where ω is the angular velocity (s−1). Electrical contact is made to the electrode by means of a brush contact (e.g., carbon-silver) (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). RDEs are mostly used in hydrodynamic techniques, for example, hydrodynamic amperometry or voltammetry.

RDEs can’t be applicable for reversal techniques because the product of the electrochemical reaction is continuously swept away from the surface of the disk (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Therefore, a concentric-ring electrode can be added to surround the disk, which produces the rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) (Fig. 6.23 ). Since in RRDE experiments, two potentials (that of the disk, E D, and that of the ring, E R) and two currents (i D and i R) are measured and reported. Moreover, the ring itself can be used alone as an electrode, which is called the rotating ring electrode (RRE).

There are only three main sources of analytical signals, which include potential, current and charge (Wang, 2006; Harvey, 2000). However, a wide variety of experimental designs can be utilized. There is a simple division, consisting of (1) bulk methods, measuring properties of the solution (e.g., conductivity); and (2) interfacial methods, investigating processes occurring at the interface of electrode-solution (pH measurements using a pH electrode) (Wang, 2006; Harvey, 2000). Here, we will only focus on interfacial electrochemical methods. In this regard, the two main types of electroanalytical measurements are potentiometric and potentiostatic methods. A classification of interfacial electrochemical methods is presented by Harvey (2000). On the basis of Harvey classifications the electrochemical methods are totally separated in: (a) static and (b) dynamic methods. The potentiometry is the main method of class “a.” The controlled current coulometry and controlled potential are the methods that they were extracted from class “b.” The controlled potential method has divided to fixed potential (b-i) and variable potential (b-ii) classes. The controlled potential coulometry and amperometry methods were extracted from “b-i.” The voltammetry method was divided into three: quiescent solution, stripping voltammetry, and stirred solution methods. The hydrodynamic voltammetry comes from stirred solution method. The cyclic voltammetry (CV), plus polarography, voltammetry, polarography and stationary electrode voltammetry are the main methods that they are located in the class of quiescent solution method (Harvey, 2000). See Fig. 6.24 for voltammetry method classification.

Potentiometry is a static (zero-current) method in which the measurement of the potential instated across a membrane leads to the information about the sample composition (e.g., analyte concentration) (Wang, 2006; Harvey, 2000). Selectivity can be developed by varying the membrane materials, which is extremely useful for direct monitoring of ionic species (e.g., protons, fluoride and potassium ions) (Wang, 2006). In potentiometry measurements, such as no current, or a negligible current flow, the sample composition remains unchanged, which makes it highly desirable for quantitative analysis (Harvey, 2000). Potentiometric measurements are applied by a potentiometer to a potential gradient between a WE and a CE. According to the point that no significant current flows, the CE can usually play the role of the RE. Potentiometric biosensors have been designed around many biologically active species, such as antibodies, bacterial particles, enzymes, and tissue, in which the biological piece is immobilized on the surface of the electrode (Harvey, 2000). The reaction of the analyte with these biological species creates a product whose concentration is measured by the mentioned electrode.

In potentiostatic techniques, the current is measured as a result of charge transfer processes at the interface of electrode-solution under dynamic (nonzero-current) conditions (Wang, 2006). To this concept, potentiostatic techniques can be used to measure any electroactive species (i.e., can be reduced or oxidized). Indirect or derivatization procedures may also lead to measuring nonelectroactive compounds. The controlled-potential techniques contain a large number of advantages, such as high sensitivity, selectivity, a wide linear range, extremely low (nanomolar) limits of detection (LODs), very small sample volumes (5–20-µL), portable and low-cost instrumentation, and a wide range of electrodes that allow working in various environments (Wang, 2006). Furthermore, selectivity may be improved via the coupling of these techniques with chromatographic or optical procedures.

As mentioned previously, in dynamic electrochemical methods, such as coulometry, voltammetry, and amperometry, the current is measured as a response of an applied potential (Wang, 2006; Harvey, 2000). We are attempting to give a general description about each technique in the following:

In coulometry, the current, created by analyte exhaustive oxidization or reduction, is measured (Harvey, 2000). This technique can be used for the quantitative analysis of various inorganic and organic compounds. There are two types of coulometry: controlled-potential and controlled-current coulometries (Harvey, 2000). The controlled-potential coulometric analyses are involved in determination of inorganic ions (e.g., trace metals and halides) and alloys analysis. The controlled-current coulometry is a more fruitful analytical method compared to controlled-potential coulometry, due to the use of mediators. The analysis of proteins can be done conveniently using controlled-current coulometry. Controlled-current coulometric techniques have been developed for a range of analytes that may be determined by conventional redox titrimetry (e.g., acid-base, precipitation, or complexation).

Chronopotentiometry is a method in which a current pulse is applied to the WE and the response potential is measured (vs RE) as a function of time (Pyun et al., 2012). Various types of chronopotentiometric techniques are named constant current, linearly rising current, current reversal, and cyclic chronopotentiometry (see Fig. 6.25 ).

In amperometry a constant potential is applied to the WE, and the response current is measured as a function of time. In amperometry, the potential is not scanned and consequently it does not lead to any voltammogram. One important application of amperometry is in the manufacture of sensors. One of the first amperometric sensors was for measurement of dissolved O2 in blood, which was developed by Qlark (1956).

As it is well known, the surface of electrodes, especially nanostructured-modified ones, have a high potential of getting damaged or made passive by analyte oxidation products that can reduce their operation and reproducibility. One of the advantages of amperometry, in this case, is evaluating the antifouling ability of the electrodes (Ping et al., 2012). Furthermore, amperometry is highly beneficial for using as a detector in flow systems or as a selective sensor to rapidly analyze a single analyte (Harvey, 2000). The amperometric sensors are also portable as potentiometric sensors, which makes them ideal for field studies.

In chronoamperometry, a stationary WE and unstirred solution are employed and a current-time curve is obtained, which reflects the variation of the concentration gradient close to the surface (Fig. 6.26 ) (Wang, 2006).

Chronoamperometry is usually used to measure the diffusion coefficient of the electroactive species or the surface area of the WE according to the Cottrell equation (Eq. (6.3)) (Wang, 2006):
