where, n, F, A, C, D, and t are the number of electrons, Faraday’s constant, the surface area, the concentration, the diffusion coefficient, and time, respectively. Chronoamperometry can also be applied for in vivo bioanalysis or studying the mechanisms of the electrode processes (Wang, 2006).

The chronocoulometry is a potential-step measurement to record the charge-time dependence (Wang, 2006). The plot of the charge versus t 1/2 is called an Anson plot and is particularly useful in order to measure the quantity of adsorbed reactants as it can separate the charges produced by the adsorbed and solution species.

Voltammetry is referred to a method in which a time-dependent potential is applied to a WE and the resulting current between the working and auxiliary electrodes is measured as a function of that potential. A plot of current versus applied potential is called a voltammogram (Harvey, 2000). Generally, in controlled-potential methods, plenty of potential excitations (e.g., a ramp, potential steps, pulse trains, a sine wave, and various combinations thereof) exist. Therefore, various voltammetric methods may be used in quantitative and qualitative analyses. The most common ones will be discussed further.

Voltammetric methods that include convection by stirring are known as hydrodynamic voltammetry (Harvey, 2000). In this method, current is considered as a function of the potential applied to a solid WE. The applied potential profile in hydrodynamic voltammetry is the same used for polarography; a linear scan or a differential pulse, and the outcome voltammograms are also identical to those for polarography, except for the lack of current oscillations due to the growth of the mercury drops. Furthermore, the hydrodynamic voltammetry is not limited to Hg electrodes (Harvey, 2000).

An approach to decrease the detection limits of voltammetric measurements is based on pulse voltammetric techniques which were introduced by Barker and Jenkins (1952). Due to the greatly improved performance of modern pulse techniques, they have supplanted classic polarography in the analytical studies. Pulse techniques are mostly based on a sampled current/potential-step measurement (Wang, 2006). The difference between the pulse voltammetric techniques is the excitation waveform and the current sampling regime (Fig. 6.28). Like polarography, pulse voltammetry techniques can be classified to normal-pulse, reverse-pulse, differential pulse, and square-wave voltammetry (SWV).

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is an highly applicable technique for measuring trace amounts of organic and inorganic species. In DPV, fixed magnitude pulses are applied to WE. In this method, the current is measured twice, once before the pulse application (t 1) and once late in the pulse life (t 2). The current difference [Δi=i(t 2)−i(t 1)] is instrumentally plotted against the applied potential. In the resulting differential-pulse voltammogram, the height of the peak current is directly proportional to the concentration of the corresponding analyte. Accordingly, DPV provides measurements at concentrations as low as 10−8 M and also information about the chemical form of analyte (oxidation states, complexation, etc.).

SWV is a large-amplitude differential voltammetry. The major advantage of SWV is its extremely fast speed (Wang, 2006). Furthermore, in SWV, both forward and reverse currents are measured (Rather et al., 2016), therefore the current increases four times higher than that of DPV leading to an increase in sensitivity.

In stripping voltammetry, the current is monitored as a function of the applied potential while the analyte is first deposited on the electrode’s surface and then removed, or “stripped,” electrochemically. This quantitative voltammetric technique is composed of three related techniques: anodic, cathodic, and adsorptive stripping voltammetry. The anodic stripping voltammetry has found the widest application. For more information in this area, we suggest any electrochemistry textbook (Harvey, 2000).

In general, the stripping method is a two-step technique (Wang, 2006). In the first step, also called deposition step, a small portion of the metal ions in the solution deposits into the electrode. The second step, i.e., stripping or measurement step, takes place by the dissolution (stripping) of the deposit. Different versions of stripping analysis can be employed, e.g., anodic stripping voltammetry, potentiometric stripping analysis, cathodic stripping voltammetry, abrasive stripping voltammetry, adsorptive stripping voltammetry and potentiometry, depending on type of deposition and measurement steps.

CV is the most widely used technique to obtain qualitative information about the electrochemical reactions. CV is often the first experiment done in an electroanalytical study. Particularly, it consists of linearly scanning the potential of a stationary WE (in an unstirred solution), applying a triangular potential waveform (Fig. 6.29A ) (Wang, 2006). Depending on the required information, single or multiple cycles may be used. During the potential sweep, the resulting current has been measured and the resulting current-potential plot (i.e., cyclic voltammogram) is reported (Fig. 6.29B). CV is also useful for quantitative purposes, based on the peaks current.

In general, the choice of which voltammetric technique to use depends on the sample’s nature, e.g., its location and the analyte’s expected concentration. An advantageous point of voltammetry for quantitative analysis is its capability to analyze more than one analyte in a single sample. When the voltammograms for two components overlap, a simultaneous analysis (besides the independent analysis) may be employed.

Currently, nanotechnology plays an important role in the field of sensors and biosensors and many other areas of science and technology. A large amount of researches demonstrated that CNMs in general, and fullerene as their first member, can effectively improve the performance and sensitivity of biosensors. Due to their unique and favorable features, in different points of chemistry, conductivity, and electrochemical properties, their application in electrochemical sensors, especially in the case of pharmaceutical compounds, is under investigation (Zhang et al., 2016; Pilehvar and De Wael, 2015).

A report of fullerene-based electrochemical sensors was about the determination of acyclovir (ACV) in pharmaceutical samples (tablets), human urine, and blood serum using DPV technique (Shetti et al., 2012). ACV is a nucleoside antiviral drug with antiviral activity in vitro against DNA viruses, such as herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, varicella zoster virus, and cytomegalovirus (O’Brien and Campoli-Richards, 1989). In phosphate buffer solution (PBS), ACV presented an irreversible oxidation peak at about 0.96V. The C60/GCE was prepared by drop-casting the dichloromethane dispersion of C60 on the surface of well-cleaned GCE, with further electrochemical reduction in KOH solution. Partial electrochemical reduction of fullerene film increases its conductivity and electrocatalytic properties (Szucs et al., 1995). The electroactive surface area of the modified electrode was measured to be higher, compared to bare GCE using CV in K3[Fe(CN)6] probe (Shetti et al., 2012). Under optimized conditions, the C60/GCE presented a linear response toward ACV concentration range of 9.0×10−8 to 6.0×10−6 M with 1.48×10−8 M LOD. They also studied the effect of various interferences, such as glucose, starch, sucrose, dextrose, citric acid, magnesium stearate, ascorbic acid (AA), lactic acid, and dopamine (DA) in the response signal of C60/GCE while measuring ACV (Shetti et al., 2012).

In current industrial and fast grown life, humans are potentially exposed to some amount of chemicals and their effects. As one of the chemical compounds, bisphenol-A (BPA) is one of the chemicals which is used in the production of polycarbonate plastic and epoxy resins (Calafat et al., 2005). BPA can be released into the environment during the production process and by leaching from the products (Calafat et al., 2005; Rather and De Wael, 2013; Völkel et al., 2002). Due to the widespread use of BPA, the potential for human exposure is expressed to be high (Calafat et al., 2005). Altered brain development, sexual differentiation behavior, reduced fertility and immune function, which could extend to future generations (Völkel et al., 2002). Therefore, determination of trace amounts in wastewater and wastewater sludge is crucial. An electrochemical sensor for determination of BPA was reported in the work of Rather and De Wael (2013) where the activated C60 molecule was immobilized on the surface of GCE and remarkable elecrocatalytic activity was observed. The sensitivity of activated C60-modified electrode was evaluated by chronocoulometry technique; higher diffusion coefficient of BPA at C60/GCE compared to bare GCE confirmed it. The decreased overpotential and the enhanced peak current was the result of voltammetry studies using C60/GCE in BPA-contained PBS. SWV was applied in order to electrochemically determine BPA in the linear concentration range of 74nM–0.23μM with LOD of 3.7nM, which was the lowest compared to all later reported methods as mentioned in Rather and De Wael (2013). They also treated the waste water containing BPA through green technology of bulk electrolysis, i.e., completely electrooxidizing –OH functional groups of BPA which leads to losing its estrogenic activity.

In order to covalently immobilize the C60 nanorods (C60 NRs) on the surface of GCE, Rather et al. (2016) proposed p-nitrophenyl diazonium salt to obtain the nitrophenyl-modified electrode (GCE–Ph–NO2) with further chemical reduction to phenylamine groups (GCE–Ph–NH2). The covalent bond was created by N–H addition reaction across the π-bond of C60. Same as the past two reports, they also used electrochemical reduction in KOH solution as the final step of electrode preparation to activate its surface enhancing the electrocatalytic behavior. The electrochemically reduced C60-based electrode (ERC60NRs–NH–Ph–GCE) was applied for ethylparaben (EP) determination over a linear concentration range of 0.01–0.52μM with a LOD of 0.0038μM in PBS and commercial cosmetic product. EP is a member of paraben family used as preservatives in cosmetic formulations (Andersen, 2008). Other members of this family consist of methylparaben, propylparaben, butylparaben, etc. They are not are not significantly toxic, but they can adsorb and metabolize in body.

A urease-immobilized functionalized fullerene potentiometric biosensor was used to catalyze the hydrolysis of urea to form alkaline reaction products (Saeedfar et al., 2013). Potentially toxic products of nitrogen metabolism in the body are converted into urea (Taylor and Vadgama, 1992). It is also driven from protein metabolism and may have evolved for the disposal of hydrogen ions. Urea synthesis from ammonia in the liver and its accurate determination is essential in various applications such as glomerular filtration rate determination, renal function testing, food science, and environmental monitoring (Saeedfar et al., 2013; Taylor and Vadgama, 1992). In order to prepare fullerene-based SPE, C60 was functionalized with carboxyl groups (to form C60-COOH) by sonication, heat, and ultraviolet (UV) radiation, instead (Saeedfar et al., 2013). Furthermore, urease enzyme was immobilized onto C60-COOH. The product was then deposited onto a pH-selective SPE containing an acrylic membrane with good adhesion. Fullerene increased the sensitivity of the potentiometric method as it was combined with urease because of its high surface area-to-volume ratio for urease immobilization. The linear dynamic concentrations of urea was achieved to be 1.2–0.042mM in this study. Furthermore, the response of the biosensor was diminished by only 5% after 140 days.

The high surface-to-volume ratio of CNTs and other unique features such as excellent electrical conductivity, high chemical stability, and robust mechanical nature e made them highly desirable for electrochemical sensors technology. The first time, CNT electrodes were constructed in the work of Britto (Britto et al., 1996) in order to study the oxidative behavior of dopamine (DA) and the results illustrated the potential of these electrodes for in vitro and in vivo DA investigations. Since then, various studies were done to electroanalyze a variety of pharmaceutical and biological compound by means of functionalized or ordinary CNTs.

For example, Asturias-Arribas et al. (2014) reported an electrochemical sensor based on CNTs for detection of cocaine in the presence of three street-sample interferences; codeine, paracetamol, and caffeine. In this study, by means of SWV, an MWCNT-modified SPE was used to measure cocaine in street samples. Cocaine is an alkaloid and widely used drug which causes addiction (Asturias-Arribas et al., 2013, 2014). It is worth mentioning that cocaine determination is crucial in fields of forensic science and criminology for considering the presence of other drugs or alcohols in biological and street samples. The proposed method determined cocaine in linear concentration range of 10–155μM, with a reproducibility of 5.6% (RSD, n=7).

In another study, a printed film based on MWCNTs was fabricated on a polyethylene terephthalate substrate during a mass flexographic printing process (Tsierkezos et al., 2014). The printing techniques are inexpensive and rapid compared to other conventional thin film producing methods. The film was applied in electrochemical biosensing through the oxidation of acetaminophen (AC), DA, and uric acid (UA) in PBS. The results demonstrated that the proposed electrode exhibits an enhanced response toward the electroactivity of these three molecules with quite good repeatability (<2.5%) and reproducibility (<3.5%). Moreover, DA and UA did not interfere with each other, which is the important factor for their simultaneous determination, especially in real samples such as human urine. DA is a key neurotransmitter which belongs to the catecholamine family and is involved in body movement and cognitive function (Wightman et al., 1988). The deficiency of dopamine levels may cause many neurological disorders, such as Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, and schizophrenia diseases (Smythies, 1999; Damier et al., 1999). UA is the primary end product of purine metabolism, a powerful antioxidant and scavenger of singlet oxygen and radicals (Ames et al., 1981). The abnormal concentration levels of UA may lead to several diseases such as hyperuricemia, gout, and pneumonia (Riches et al., 2009). The MWCNT/PET electrode disposed a simple, stable, flexible, sensitive, and low-priced sensor for determination of these biologically important molecules.

A MWCNTs-modified PGE was used for the determination of diclofenac sodium (DIC) in different mediums, such as acetate buffer solution (ABS), commercial tablets, and urine samples (Fard et al., 2016). Diclofenac is the first nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drug (NSAID) (Small, 1989). This derivative of phenylacetic acid has intensive analgesic, antipyretic, and antiinflammatory activities. It is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract and metabolized in the liver. DIC is well tolerated and rarely produces gastrointestinal ulcerations or other side-effects, in comparison with other NSAIDs (Fard et al., 2016; Small, 1989). It is used in the treatment of painful and inflammatory conditions and also effectively enhances action in patients with rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis. Under optimized conditions in Alipour’s report (Fard et al., 2016), a calibration graph driven from DVP study, described the linear relation between the anodic peak currents and DIC concentration from 0.047 to 12.95μM with a LOD of 0.017μM (S/N=3) using MWCNTs/PGE.

High electrical conductivity, large surface area, basal-plane defect sites, biocompatibility and many other great properties of graphene, make it highly desirable and applicable in electrochemical devices especially biosensors (Shang et al., 2008; Kuila et al., 2011).

A SPE containing graphene ink was prepared in the study of Ping and collages in order to simultaneously determine ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid by DPV technique (Ping et al., 2012). AA, known as vitamin C, is commonly used as an antioxidant in food, animal feed, pharmaceutical formulations, and cosmetic applications (André et al., 2010; Padayatty et al., 2003). It also plays an important role in many physiological and biochemical processes, such as free radical scavenging, reduction of a reactive free radical with formation of a less reactive compound; cancer prevention; and immunity improvement. The deficiency of AA may result in cataract, scurvy, and atherosclerosis (Padayatty et al., 2003). This biomolecule is often coexisted with dopamine and uric acid in physiological fluids at different concentrations and their determination is an important topic in all fields of biomedical chemistry, neurochemistry, and pathological research. Reproducibility, wide potential window, brilliant electrocatalytic activity, low background current, stability, antifouling ability, and fast electron transfer kinetics are of the great features of the graphene-based electrode proposed in this study (Ping et al., 2012). The electrode was prepared by modified Hummers-synthesized graphene oxide (Hummers and Offeman, 1958) and n-butylpyridinium hexafluorophosphate (BPFP) ionic liquid (IL)-doped ink was printed in a poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) substrate. ILs are the combination of large organic cations and relatively small anions which melt around or below 100°C (Abo-Hamad et al., 2016; Tunckol et al., 2012). Low toxicity, high electric and ionic conductivity, low volatility, and high solvation ability are of the great features of ILs which make them suitable to be considered as “green media” in chemical systems (Tunckol et al., 2012; Park and Kazlauskas, 2003). Besides being used as solvent for organic synthesis, ILs have a variety of applications in electrochemical synthesis, electrochemical devices, and electrochemical analysis in order to modify electrodes or electrochemical biosensors (Torimoto et al., 2010; Opallo and Lesniewski, 2011). Keeping this in mind, and considering the high density of edge-plane-like defective sites on chemically reduced graphene oxide (RGO) that can remarkably increase the electron transfer kinetics and significantly diminish the overpotential (Ping et al., 2012), make the sensor applicable toward the monitoring of simultaneous electrochemical oxidation of the three mentioned compounds. They were determined with the linear response ranges and detections limits (S/N=3) of 4.0–4500μM (LOD=0.95μM), 0.5–2000μM (LOD=0.12μM), and 0.8–2500μM (LOD=0.20μM), respectively, using screen-printed graphene electrode (Ping et al., 2012). Furthermore, the applicability of the electrode for biological sample analysis was investigated which presented high selectivity and high sensitivity for simultaneous determination of these three compounds. Other studies in simultaneous determination of these molecules using CNMs-based electrodes can be find on (Bi et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2013; Lete et al., 2015). Furthermore, there are also reports of graphene-based modified electrodes for detection of DA in presence of other compounds, such as epinephrine (Ma et al., 2012).

In a study of hydrochlorothiazide (HCT) determination, (6-chloro-3,4-dihydro-l,2,4-benzo-thiadiazine-7-sulfonamide-l,I-dioxide), in pharmaceutical dosage forms and clinical samples, a high-quality graphene–modified electrode (H-GRE) was employed using adsorptive stripping differential pulse voltammetry (AdSDPV) technique (Er et al., 2015). Instantly, GO was prepared by Hummers’ method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958) and then converted to H-GR through 2h reflux at 130°C in concentrated H3PO4 in a boiling flask. H-GRE was prepared by drop-casting 5μL H-GR (1mg/mL containing 0.25% Nafion) at the surface of GCE. The applied AdSDPV at the surface of bare GCE and H-GRE represented 2.8 times bigger anodic peak and slightly cathodic shift for the modified electrode. The authors demonstrated that the adsorption of HCT to the H-GRE was attributed to the interaction of NH2 functional groups in HCT and COOH groups presented at the edges of the H-GR. The modified electrode performed excellent electrical conductivity, large surface area, and enhanced sensitivity toward the HCT oxidation. Therefore, the optimum linear response was found to be 0.1–48.0μM with a detection limit (based on S/N=3) of 19nM. They have also successfully determined the HTC value in pharmaceutical tablets (RSD=2.32%) and human blood plasma (RSD=4.02%) (Er et al., 2015).

Rabieh et al. (2015) reported the electrochemical growth of GNs in situ on the surface of graphite electrode and its application for prazosin hydrochloride (PRA) determination using AdSDPV in urine samples and tablets. PRA is an antihypertensive agent belonging to α-adrenergic blocker which can lower blood pressure (Koshy et al., 1977; Cohen, 1970). Moreover, it is proved that α-blockers have antitumor effect on prostate cancer cells (Tahmatzopoulos et al., 2004). The two-step electrosynthesis of GNs on the surface of graphite electrode was first published by Zeng et al. (2011). During the first step, the intercalation and exfoliation of graphite to graphene oxide takes place. And later, by the second step, during the negative potentials scan, deoxygenation of functional groups leads the surface of the electrode to be modified by GNs. In the case of PRA determination by GNs/graphite electrode, the oxidation peak of PRA was linearly related to its concentration in the range of 0.09–100μM. The LOD of 0.02μM and RSD of 3.8% for 10μM solution of PRA was obtained with good recovery (Rabieh et al., 2015).

Acetylsalicylic acid (ASA), or aspirin, is another important drug which is highly used in treatment of high blood pressure, especially in patients with hypertension (Hansson et al., 1998). The electrochemical detection of ASA using graphene modified-SPE in drug samples and human oral fluid was investigated in the work of Kruanetr et al. (2015) and proposing its electrooxidation mechanism.
Other CNMs was also investigated in electrochemical sensing of pharmaceutical and biological compounds. Some recent studies of this case will be mentioned here.

4-nm-sized uniform monodisperse watersoluble C-dots were synthesized from glucose and employed to modify GCE for DA determination in a study by Li et al. (2015). The calibration curve represented the linear relation of peak current in DPV with DA concentration from 1.5×10−7 to 1.5×10−4 M with LOD of 2.6×10−8 M. Furthermore, the proposed sensor was employed to determine DA in human plasma without interferences from UA and AA. Carbon dots/GCE also performed acceptable stability and high precision.

An electrochemical sensor with OMC-modified GCE (OMC/GCE) using SWV technique was proposed to determine prednisolone (Munyentwali and Zhu, 2015). Prednisolone is a corticosteroid abused for doping in sports and the suggested method can provide a rapid and simple way of doping detection, even at the sites of competitive games. However, it is ordinarily used in treatment of a wide variety of diseases, including arthritis, asthma, allergy, hepatitis, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, and many other hematological, cardiac, dermal, neurological, gastrointestinal diseases, many inflammatory states, and also at very high doses for severe shock treatments (Vogt et al., 2007). The OMC/GCE perfectly responded toward the electrochemical reduction of prednisolone, displayed in increased reduction peak currents and lowered reduction potential, compared to the bare GCE (Munyentwali and Zhu, 2015). The peak current demonstrated a linear relationship with the concentration of prednisolone in the range of 0.06–40µM. The method proposed a sensitivity of 0.644µA/µM and the LOD of 0.057µM. The determination of prednisolone in pharmaceutical injection and spiked human urine was also investigated without any affect from common urine metabolites interferences.

WEs can also be modified with nanocomposites containing different types of CNMs, with other materials such as nanostructures of metals and metal oxides, conducting polymers, ILs, etc. to measure pharmaceutical compounds. Here, we review these kinds of reports.

Lately, Mazloum-Ardakani and his team proposed a nanocomposite containing fullerene-functionalized CNTs in order to measure levodopa (LD) and AC at the surface of GCE (Mazloum-Ardakani et al., 2016). They proposed that the available porosity and surface area of this nanocomposite is highly beneficial for diffusion and electrolysis of various materials during electrochemical experiments (Mazloum-Ardakani et al., 2016). LD, (3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine), is an amino acid which is used by brain in order to produce a neurotransmitters, such as DA, norepinephrine and catecholamines. It is also used as a drug to treat patients with Parkinson’s disease (Katzenschlager and Poewe, 2014). However, studies show that higher doses of LD may cause aggressiveness (Goodwin, 1971). Long-term patient treatment with LD unfortunately can cause side effects like gastritis, abnormal movements, and psychiatric problems, mostly depression (Goodwin, 1971). Therefore analyzing the amount of levodopa in biological samples is critical. Moreover, as the patients with Parkinson’s disease mostly use pain killers such as AC, this study was focused on electrochemical analysis of these two compounds especially for better medicinal results and lower toxicity (Mazloum-Ardakani et al., 2016). As the oxidation peak potential of LD and AC overlaps at the surface of bare electrode, this study proposed C60-CNT/GCE as an electrochemical sensor for determination of LD (Mazloum-Ardakani et al., 2016). Furthermore, DPV technique was applied to detect LD in the presence of AC; LOD of 0.42µM was obtained. The performance of the mentioned electrode was also investigated for simultaneous determination of both compounds in real sample. The study suggest that in the proposed nanocomposite, C60 nanoparticles were strongly bonded to CNTs which caused the synergetic electrical conductivity and electrocatalytical capability toward the oxidation of LD for C60-CNT/GCE in comparison to CNT/GCE and bare GCE. C60-CNT/GCE represented a negatively shifted LD oxidative peak with higher current, compared to both other electrodes. Study indicates that the presence of C60 molecules on the surface of CNTs provide increment both in surface area and porosity of the electrode which causes better electrochemical responses. Moreover, the rate constant (k=9.196×102 M−1 s−1) which was driven from chronoamperometric studies also proved the high electrocatalytical capability of C60-CNT/GCE in oxidizing LD. This report investigated LD concentration in the range of 0.5–2000μM in PBS (pH=7) by the champion electrode; LOD of 0.035µM was gained. For real sample analysis, they used tablets containing 150mg of levodopa, called Madopar. They were used in detecting the amount of LD both in absence and presence of AC by the nanocomposite-modified electrode (Mazloum-Ardakani et al., 2016).

In 2015, a glucose biosensor was fabricated based on RGO and C60 composite by Thirumalraj et al. (2015). During the first step, the GO–C60 nanocomposite was prepared by simple sonication of GO–C60 (1:2) mixture and an RRDE was modified by drop-casting the nanocomposite on its surface. Furthermore, in the second step, the electrochemical reduction was employed to convert it into RGO-C60. This denoted the advantage of electrochemical technique in comparison with other reduction methods in the case of retaining some oxygen-containing functionality on the carbon basal plane, which highly affect properties of the produced nanocomposite. Finally, in the third step, 6µL of glucose oxidase (GOx) solution was drop-coated onto the RGO–C60/RRDE. The high specificity of GOx made the electrode act as a selective glucose biosensors. Under optimum conditions, the amperometry response of GOx/RGO–C60/RRDE was linear toward the variation of glucose concentration from 0.1 to 12.5mM with a response time of 3s. The LOD was calculated to be 35µM with a high sensitivity of 55.97µA/mM per cm2. In addition, the fabricated biosensor was applied for the detection of glucose in the human blood serum samples (recovery range of 97.5%).

Later in 2016, the same team reported the use of highly stable C60 wrapped GO nanocomposite electrode material to determine DA in rat brain and pharmaceutical samples (Thirumalraj et al., 2016). Again, the nanocomposite was prepared by sonication of C60 in GO solution and drop-casted on the surface of GCE, just as their previous report (Thirumalraj et al., 2015). However, in a later report, DA was detected in the linear response range of 0.02–73.5µM with the detection limit of 0.008µM based on 3S with a sensitivity of 4.23µA/µM1 per cm2 employing DPV (Thirumalraj et al., 2016). Furthermore, the results obtained in this study indicated that GO–C60/GCE can be employed for the selective detection of DA in the presence of potentially interferences such as UA, glucose, epinephrine, norepinephrine, AA, serotonin (5-HT), melatonin and thyroxine. Finally, the practical applicability of the modified electrode was investigated in rat brain (recovery 101.8%) and commercial DA injection (recovery 94.0%–98.0%) samples using DPV technique. The DA concentration in rat brain sample was found to be 40nM in this report.

A study took place in order to determine rosuvastatin calcium (RC) using a composite of vertically aligned carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide (VACNT-GO) on a copper plate WE and applying square-wave adsorptive anodic stripping voltammetry (SWAdASV) (Silva et al., 2015). The VACNT-GO electrode was prepared using a microwave-assisted chemical vapor deposition (MWCVD) method followed by oxygen plasma etching. The proposed electrode presented superhydrophilic behavior, fast charge transfer, and low working overpotential; it was highly beneficial for electrochemical analysis. The analytical curve was linear in RC concentration, in the range of 0.48–46.72μmol/L with the LOD value of 0.06μmol/L. The developed SWAdASV method was applied for quantification of RC in commercial pharmaceutical samples, and, artificial human urine and serum samples (Silva et al., 2015).

Simultaneous determination of AA, DA and UA was also investigated by Pt nanosheets-C60 nanocomposite decorated GCE (Pt NSs/C60/GCE) using DPV and amperometric measurements (Zhang et al., 2015). The large effective electroactive surface area and the synergistic effect between Pt NSs and C60, led to a better response toward the simultaneous determination of these three compounds, compared to bare GCE, C60/GCE and Pt NSs/GCE. It needs to be mentioned that the anodic peaks of AA and DA in the mixtures on bare GCE and C60/GCE were significantly overlapped. Therefore, Pt NSs/C60/GCE was chosen as a promising electrochemical biosensor for simultaneous determination of DA, AA, and DA. The experiment was done on linear concentrations of 10–1800μM (AA), 0.5–211.5μM (DA), and 9.5–1187μM (UA), which has obtained the LODs of 0.43μM, 0.07μM and 0.63μM for AA, DA and UA, respectively. Results driven from this study show lower peak potentials separation and shorter linear detection ranges but lower detection limits compared to the report GO/SPE (Ping et al., 2012), which was reviewed in Section 4.3.

Yan and his team proposed an electrochemical sensor based on a molecularly imprinted film (MIF) with an SPE for the simultaneous detection of psychotropic drugs (Zang et al., 2013). An MIF was synthesized by electrochemical polymerization employing methcathinone and cathinone as the templates with pyrrole monomer. The electrical conductivity of the SPE was enhanced by the NH2-graphene (NG). The MIP/NG/SPE showed rapid adsorption kinetics, and significant selectivity, reproducibility and repeatability. Under optimal conditions, this method provided a wide linear range of 4.9×10−6 to 9.8×10−3 μg/mL and 1.5×10−5 to 1.1×10−2 μg/mL with the detection limits (S/N=3) down to 3.3 and 8.9pg/mL for methcathinone and cathinone, respectively. This proposed electrochemical sensor array was successful to simultaneously detect methcathinone and cathinone in practical serum samples.

A review of structure, properties, and applications of CNMs definitely proves their effective roles in any science fields since their discovery. The unique, optical, electrical, and chemical properties, along with their ecofriendly and cost-effective nature, make them highly suitable to electrochemistry. It is well known that electrochemical techniques provide accuracy, portability, rapid response, sensitivity, selectivity, and low cost in the determination of any electroactive material. As electrochemical reactions take part at the interface of electrode/electrolyte, the role of the electrode material to effectively catalyze the reaction is extremely important. Wide potential window, high conductivity, chemical and electrochemical inertness, low capacitance, low overvoltage, biocompatibility, ease of modification, and low cost are the features that CNMs, as promising electrode materials provide for the electrochemical devices. These points and high accuracy, high precision, great reproducibility, high repeatability and excellent stability make them pioneers for electroanalysis and sensing purposes. As a result, they have been extensively used in determination of compounds for environmental, forensic, biological or pharmaceutical applications.
